mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Vietnam on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the south-east. It is a long thin country shaped a bit like the letter S 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What sea touches Vietnam?
mapQuestions.answer: The South China Sea, which Vietnam calls the "East Sea". Vietnam has over 3,200km of coastline.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Vietnam?
mapQuestions.answer: Hanoi in the north. The biggest city is actually Ho Chi Minh City (formerly Saigon) in the south, but Hanoi is the political capital.

mapQuestions.question: Which countries border Vietnam?
mapQuestions.answer: China to the north, Laos and Cambodia to the west. Vietnam shares a 1,300km border with China.

mapQuestions.question: What is the longest river in Vietnam?
mapQuestions.answer: The Mekong, which starts in the mountains of Tibet, flows through six countries, and ends in a huge delta of nine river mouths in southern Vietnam.

foods.name: Phở
foods.description: A clear beef broth simmered for hours with star anise, cinnamon and ginger, poured over rice noodles with thin slices of beef, fresh herbs and lime. The national dish. Eaten for breakfast.

foods.name: Bánh mì
foods.description: A crispy baguette (a French leftover) split open and stuffed with pâté, pickled carrots, fresh coriander, chilli, cucumber, and a meat of your choice. The world's best sandwich, fight us.

foods.name: Bún chả
foods.description: Grilled pork patties served in a sweet-sour fish sauce dip with cold rice noodles and a huge plate of fresh herbs. Hanoi's most famous lunch. Barack Obama ate this in 2016 and the place is now a shrine.

foods.name: Gỏi cuốn
foods.description: Fresh spring rolls wrapped in soft transparent rice paper, with prawns, pork, noodles and herbs. Dipped in peanut sauce. Light, fresh and addictive.

foods.name: Cao lầu
foods.description: A noodle dish you can only get in the town of Hoi An. Thick chewy noodles made with water from a specific local well, topped with pork, herbs, and crispy crackers. Nobody can quite replicate it elsewhere.

foods.name: Cà phê trứng
foods.description: Egg coffee. Strong Vietnamese coffee topped with whipped egg yolk and condensed milk that turns into a thick foam like a custard. Invented in Hanoi when milk was scarce. Sounds wrong, tastes magical.

scavengerItems.label: A scooter carrying a ridiculous load (a fridge, a family of five, a stack of chickens)
A "nón lá" (conical leaf hat) on someone working in a field or selling fruit
A rice paddy with green shoots growing in the water
A water buffalo in or near a paddy field
A bánh mì cart on a street corner
A coloured silk lantern hanging outside a shop
Someone carrying two huge baskets balanced on a pole across their shoulders
A market that started before sunrise
A whole roundabout where no one stops for anyone, somehow it works
The red flag with a yellow star (try counting how many you see in a day)
A small family ancestor shrine in a shop or restaurant
A train passing inches from people's front doors on Hanoi's "Train Street"

geoMatches.description: The northern capital. Lakeside parks, old French quarters, lantern-lit streets in the Old Quarter, and a chaotic-but-friendly motorbike river.
The southern megacity, still called Saigon by many. Skyscrapers next to old French villas, the best street food in the country, hot all year round.
A small old port town in the middle. Streets lit by hundreds of silk lanterns every night, tailor shops on every corner, and a quiet river flowing through.
Around 1,600 limestone islands rising straight out of emerald water in the north. The name means "where the dragon descends". You sleep on a boat and wake up between cliffs.
High up in the northern mountains. Terraced rice fields step down whole hillsides, with hill-tribe villages (Hmong, Dao, Tay) tucked into the valleys.
A huge green flatland of rivers, canals and floating markets in the far south. Most people travel by boat and almost every meal includes fish from the river.

senses.smell: Pho broth simmering at 6am, lime and fresh coriander, motorbike exhaust, jasmine flowers, charcoal grills, rain on hot tarmac, ground coffee everywhere…
senses.hear: A thousand motorbike horns at once, "Xin chào!", chopsticks on bowls, water splashing in a paddy, the singsong six-tone Vietnamese language, gentle rain on tin roofs…
senses.taste: Hot beef broth, fresh herbs by the handful, lime squeezed over everything, the punch of fish sauce, salty sweet caramel pork, strong coffee with condensed milk…
senses.feel: Sticky tropical heat, plastic stools so low your knees are by your ears, the cool of an air-conditioned café, a scooter ride that is too fast, soft river breeze…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How do scooters carry SO much stuff?
stories.body: Vietnam has around 70 million scooters for 100 million people. People learn to drive them at 16, and a scooter is the family car, lorry, school run and removal van all in one. You will see one scooter carrying a fridge. Another with a flat-screen TV strapped on. A whole family of five (dad driving, mum on the back, two kids in front, baby in the middle). A stack of live chickens. Twenty tubs of paint. A pane of glass. The all-time record sighting: a man with a full-size sofa balanced across the back seat. The unwritten rule of crossing a Vietnamese street: just walk slowly and steadily, do not stop, do not run, and the river of scooters will flow around you.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could only ever own one vehicle for the rest of your life and it had to do EVERYTHING, what would you choose?

stories.question: How did 1,600 islands appear in the sea?
stories.body: Halong Bay in the north is one of the most jaw-dropping places on earth. Around 1,600 jagged limestone islands rise straight out of jade-green water. Geologists say the islands were formed over 500 million years by rising and falling sea levels eroding limestone. The Vietnamese legend is better: long ago, the country was attacked by invaders from the north. The gods sent a family of dragons to defend Vietnam, and the dragons spat out jewels and jade that turned into the islands, forming a wall against the enemy. The dragons then liked it so much they stayed, and Halong literally means "where the dragon descends".

stories.question: Why does the whole town glow with lanterns at night?
stories.body: Hoi An is a small old town on the coast in the middle of Vietnam. For 400 years it was a busy port where merchants from China, Japan, India and Europe traded silk and spices. They left behind a mix of architectures and traditions, but the most beautiful is the lantern. Every night the town turns off most of its street lights and the whole place glows with thousands of silk lanterns in every colour. On the full moon, motor traffic is banned, candles float down the river, and people release floating lanterns from little paper boats. It feels like time has stopped.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could pick one night a month for your town to turn off all street lights and decorate with something, what would it be?

stories.location: 📍 Cu Chi, near Ho Chi Minh City
stories.question: What were the Cu Chi tunnels?
stories.body: During the Vietnam War (1955-1975), called the "American War" here, Vietnamese fighters in the south dug a network of tunnels stretching about 250km underground. The tunnels had hospitals, kitchens, sleeping areas, and weapon stores, all 3 to 10 metres beneath the ground. Whole families lived down there for years. The tunnels were tiny (often the width of a person's shoulders), invisibly camouflaged, and protected by hidden traps. Bigger American soldiers could not fit. Today you can crawl through small widened sections that have been opened for visitors. It is hot, narrow, dark and very hard to imagine living there.
stories.thinkingQuestion: The Cu Chi tunnels show what humans will do when their backs are against the wall. Have you ever solved a problem by going around it in a really creative way?

stories.question: Why is Vietnam now one of the youngest countries on earth?
stories.body: After the Vietnam War ended in 1975, Vietnam had been fighting for almost 30 years and was one of the poorest countries in the world. From 1986 the government reformed the economy, and now Vietnam is one of the fastest-growing countries on the planet. Over half of the 100 million Vietnamese are under 35 years old. The average age is just 33. There are more young people, students and university graduates being born here every year than almost anywhere else. Walk around Hanoi or Saigon in the evening and the streets are full of teenagers and twenty-somethings, on scooters, in cafés, starting businesses.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Vietnam so far?
How many scooters can you count on one road in a minute? (Properly try it.)
Have you tried egg coffee? On a scale of 1 to 10, was it weirder or better than you expected?
What is one thing the Vietnamese seem to do better than back home?
Have you found your favourite Vietnamese place yet? What is it?
The Vietnamese have lived through huge wars and now their country is full of young people building new things. What kind of country would you want to build if you could start over?

wordSearch.hint: Vietnamese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Water buffalo
animals.description: Ploughing rice paddies all over the country. Often ridden home by farm kids.

animals.name: Vietnamese street dog
animals.description: The country has been working hard to end the dog-meat trade — most are pets now.

animals.name: Tokay gecko
animals.description: Loud blue-grey-with-orange-spots gecko on house walls at night. Calls "to-kay! to-kay!".

animals.name: Free-range chicken
animals.description: Crossing every village road. Will give you the side-eye.

animals.name: Cat Ba langur
animals.description: Critically endangered orange-yellow monkey, only on Cat Ba island in Halong Bay.

animals.name: Saola
animals.description: Asian "unicorn" antelope-like creature, only discovered in 1992. So rare you basically won't see one — but try.
